textPlain: I'm sure it's nearly an academic distinction, but:> Basically, for any given region, we find its highest point and assume that there is a perfectly placed sibling peak of the same height that is mutually visible.Shouldn't you always add 335km to the horizon distance to account for the possibility of Everest (i.e. a taller sibling peak) being on the other side of the horizon?

I was recently looking into this for roguelike-like visibility calculations. The term I came across was "isovist" not "viewshed", but they seem identical? The former appears to be used a lot in architecture.Calculating a 2d isovist is (relatively) simple, for 3d it seems much more complex. Is this simplified by the fact that it's dealing with a height map?

Related, there’s a Reddit post tracking the furthest photos of the Sydney skyline:https://www.reddit.com/r/sydney/comments/1lzke5t/the_actual_...Google Earth map: https://earth.google.com/web/@-32.84964163,149.89452785,590....

Since you asked about alternate packing solutions:https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=43305920I don't know enough about the subject to have any useful suggestions, but I'd be interested in your take on how a project like yours would work differently if you were to choose a different solution.
If you consider atmospheric refraction it might be more complicated...

There's a nearly 450 km photo of the Ecrins from the Pyrenees!https://beyondrange.wordpress.com/2016/08/03/pic-de-finestre...That's a cool thing to track, I've been surprised by the places I've seen the Seattle skyline reflected on the water near sundown. No crazy distances, but there's a small time period where it flashes into view.
